data.title: Lonza: Structural Growth Intact, CHI Exit Strengthens Focus, Recent Weakness Reinforces Entry Opportunity
data.description:
A year after our last update, we are back to comment on Lonza Group AG (LZAGY) (LZAGF). This was very good timing (we buy rated Lonza just after liberation day, with a follow-up note on the company's ability to navigate tariff turmoil due to its extensive industrial capacity in the US); unfortunately, the company's share price declined significantly due to the escalation in the Middle East (Fig. 1).

Our buy rating was also backed by a recovery in margins, upside in US manufacturing onshoring capacity, and an attractive valuation vs. its historical average. With earnings growth set to outpace revenue, the overall risk-reward profile was supportive of remaining buyers. Here at the Lab, we believe this negative market moment offers (once again) an opportunity to increase Lonza's exposure.

Fig. 1

Lonza Results and Our Positive Stance

The company reported its earnings at the end of January. We will be very brief, just to recap. In 2025, the company reported top-line sales of CHF 6.5 billion and core EBITDA of CHF 2.1 billion (Fig. 2). Sales increased by more than 20%, while the EBITDA margin was 140 basis points higher than in 2024. Lonza reported better numbers than its Capital Market Day outlook. H2 EBITDA was supported by solid momentum in ADS due to 1) project growth and 2) operating leverage on higher sales. Still at the divisional level, H2 EBITDA was slightly offset by lower Biologics sales and by Specialized Modalities, which recovered, but segment turnover remains soft, down -3% year over year. The Specialized Modalities segment also saw a minus 8.3% in EBITDA compared to last year.

On a positive note, Lonza reported a solid improvement in FCF generation from CHF 232 million to CHF 545 million. This was due to higher earnings and lower CAPEX investment. So, after two consecutive years of flat DPS, the company proposed a DPS of CHF 5 (Fig. 3), representing a 25% year-on-year increase.

Fig. 2

Fig. 3

Why are we Positive?

It is worth noting that Lonza reported a solid Fiscal Year 2025 and beat Wall Street expectations by 1% on sales and 4% on EBITDA. The company is progressing in a new "simplified and streamlined operating model." Here at the Lab, we reported how growth was more skewed at the margin level, and this is what has happened. Lonza EBITDA growth was 35.7% vs. a top-line sales increase of 21.7%. That said, we report a positive CAPEX decline trajectory as a % of sales in the coming years (Fig. 4) and a solid 2026 outlook (Fig. 5). In numbers, the implied FY26 sales were narrower, in the 11-12% range. There is an explanation for margin expansion when the forecast is above 32%. This is a supportive take to price in. Additionally, during the Q&A on the analysts' call, we got the sense that H1 results will be supportive, so Lonza's equity story is not postponed to H2 2026. This is also positive considering an easy H1 2024 comp.

Fig. 4

Fig. 5

More importantly, and still aligned with Lonza's priority of simplifying its business model, in early March, Lonza announced the divestment of the CHI division to Lone Star. The company hosted an analyst call later, and we reported that the transaction is valued at CHF 2.3 billion and will generate CHF 1.7 billion in cash proceeds. Lonza will retain a 40% minority equity stake with a minimum hurdle rate of return. If reached, the company will have a full exit of CHF 3 billion in 5-7 years. As reported by the company, Lonza completed "its Transformation to a Pure-Play CDMO." Additionally, the company confirmed its 2026 guidance. At first glance, the proceeds appear slightly light; however, this removes uncertainty around exit, and the company communicated a CHF 500 million return to shareholders via a buyback. This represents around 1.5% of Lonza's current market cap, and we believe it is a welcome signal to investors.

While we remain positive about the broader Pharma industry, it is worth noting that the sector is approaching an unprecedented patent cliff in the coming years (in detail, there is an expectation of losing $300 billion in pharmaceutical sales, one-sixth of the industry, by 2030). In this context, Lonza’s large-scale investments across the global CDMO platform position the company particularly well (Fig. 6), as the expected shift toward biologics will require significant additional manufacturing capacity. This is why Lonza has an organic growth model embedded in its operating model to achieve sales growth above 10% (higher than the pharma industry; Fig. 7).

Fig. 6

Fig. 7

Earnings Change and Valuation

In our forecast, we are reducing CHI sales and EBITDA. This division generated sales of CHF 1 billion and a core EBITDA of CHF 270 million in 2025. That said, Lonza still estimated a return in the high single digits in sales with a core EBITDA margin above 32%. Therefore, this exit is slightly accretive to Lonza EBITDA. In our previous analysis, we forecasted an EPS of CHF 15.4, which was consistent with the actual result of CHF 15.03 (Fig. 8). Considering CHI disposal, we see sales of CHF 7 billion with a 32% EBITDA margin. Lonza decided to book CHF 1.3 billion in CHI disposal impairment in 2025. Therefore, we see limited P&L volatility at this stage. With a lower D&A expectation on the back of lower CAPEX and net financial results of -CHF 150 million (within the company's mid-guidance range—Fig. 9), our EBIT reached CHF 1.61 billion, with a net profit of CHF 1.19 billion. Last time, Lonza's debt was CHF 4 billion; now it is estimated at CHF 3.2 billion (excluding the latest disposal). For the valuation, we used an unchanged EV/EBITDA multiple of 21x. This EV/EBITDA is aligned with its 5-year historical average. In our estimates, we derive an enterprise value of CHF 47 billion with an equity value of CHF 43.84 billion. However, there is a 1.5% upside from the buyback and some deleverage to consider, with an additional CHF 1 billion (+2.8%). There is a stock price upside of 23.5%, which is combined with a dividend yield of 1%, a buyback yield of 1.5%, and the above de-leverage. We continue to buy rated Lonza at a target price of CHF 620 per share ($77.4 in ADR terms).

Fig. 8

Fig. 9

Risks

Lonza's downside risks include supply-and-demand imbalances in biologics, with overcapacity in some areas. FX risks given Lonza US sales. Other company risks include higher funding costs, product failure, and higher CAPEX. The company is also subject to regulatory barriers, given the pharma industry's high regulatory standards. Lonza is well-priced at multiple levels. Therefore, we report a valuation risk given the company/industry's high multiple.

Conclusion

Lonza reported solid 2025 numbers and completed a full transformation. It is set to deliver growth ahead in sales and EBITDA margin. With an ongoing buyback and multiple layers of upside, we remain buyers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CGDG: Dividend Growth ETF That Offers Global Protection
data.description:
Overview

With the continued pullback in the indices, it can be scary to add to positions right now. However, there are plenty of high-quality ETFs that offer better price resilience than the indices because of their core focus on dividend-paying companies. This is where the Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF (CGDG) comes in. CGDG not only provides exposure to some of the highest-quality dividend companies, but it also diversifies its focus to global companies with primary operations outside of the U.S. With rising global tensions, uncertainty around interest rates, and an uptick in unemployment, I wanted to provide some updated insights on CGDG and its overall value proposition.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CGDG's share price has increased by nearly 15%, which is impressive considering the recent pullback in the market. When including all dividends paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 16.4% over the same time frame. CGDG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 2%. While the starting yield isn't anything to get excited about, the magic is in the positive dividend growth potential over the next few years. I believe that patient investors will be able to see their annual dividend income effortlessly grow over time.

At the time of my last coverage, I issued a buy rating due to the global exposure away from the U.S. markets. Since then, the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 due to the recent pullback. At the time of my last coverage, I cited that the lack of exposure to specific growth companies could be a weakness. At the turn of the market cycle, it turns out that this weakness has actually become one of the reasons why CGDG can outperform over the next twelve months. If the start of 2026 is an indication of how the rest of the market will go, I believe that CGDG is an efficient holding for investors that may have a lower risk tolerance to uncertainty and volatility.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CGDG has total assets under management of $4.46B that are spread across a diverse range of dividend-paying positions. The key differentiator in CGDG's strategy is the fact that it takes a multi-manager approach to building out its portfolio. This means there are different investment outlooks being compiled into the fund to prevent any sort of biases. So when one manager thinks to keep exposure to U.S. semiconductors, another manager may think to offset this with industrial exposure. Furthermore, the fund provides direct exposure to equities that have primary operations outside of the U.S. The portfolio's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • U.S. Equities: 50.8%
  • Non-U.S. Equities: 46.6%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 2.6%

When looking at the portfolio allocation by sector, we can see that CGDG is most heavily allocated to financials at 19.09% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to technology and industrials, which account for 12.58% and 11.91% of assets. The fund doesn't aim to mirror the S&P 500 and instead chooses to focus on high-quality dividend-paying companies. The selection process avoids companies that are considered yield traps. Therefore, management focuses on positions with attractive forecasted dividend growth potential, positive payout history, and an attractive current yield.

Since CGDG is actively managed, the fund has a higher-than-average expense ratio of 0.47%. However, this may be worth it if the fund is capable of outperforming during periods of uncertainty or declines. The actively managed approach means that there is no set rebalancing schedule. This means investors are relying on management being proactive enough to swap out underperformers and add defensive names when possible. I believe this approach is better than being passively locked into a fixed rebalancing schedule because this means that CGDG can rotate out of underperformers quickly.

When looking at the top holdings within CGDG, we can see that investors are getting exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. For instance, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is the largest individual position at 3.79% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other high-quality positions, including Broadcom (AVGO), Philip Morris International (PM), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Welltower (WELL), just to name a few. It's also notable that CGDG still provides an efficient allocation to technology companies that are capable of providing rapid growth and participating in the expansion of AI.

Defensive Outlook

When I last covered CGDG, I had an issue with the fact that it lacked exposure to meaningful growth positions, such as Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META). These are high-quality businesses that continue to rapidly grow earnings over time, and they pay out a small dividend. While these technology companies were taking off last year, the story has rapidly changed, and CGDG's lack of exposure has become one of its benefits. In fact, the selloff of these technology companies is one of the main drivers behind the pullback of the indices over the last few months.

Sentiment in the market changes rapidly and can be a powerful force. A year ago, any mention of Artificial Intelligence on an earnings call resulted in a stock trending higher. Today, the market doesn't have the same perspective on AI. Advances in the AI sector have grown so rapidly that investors are now concerned that the moat for many software companies is under the threat of being eliminated. When you add on the major capex spending levels, the market is now questioning whether or not these investments will actually lead to higher baseline growth in earnings.

To demonstrate the strength of CGDG's approach, we can measure its performance on a YTD basis. The State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has declined by more than 7% on a YTD basis. However, CGDG has only declined by about 1% over the same time frame due to the lack of technology exposure. This proves that management's strategy for stock selection and rotating out of vulnerable positions can be effective. If the indices continue to experience headwinds, I anticipate that CGDG will continue to preserve capital for investors.

Furthermore, management was able to build a portfolio of positions that are undervalued from a price-to-earnings ratio. CGDG now trades with a price-to-earnings ratio of 17.4x, which undercuts the S&P 500's price-to-earnings ratio of ~27.16x. Furthermore, the portfolio has a sizeable allocation to defensive sectors like industrials, utilities, and consumer staples that tend to have easy-to-estimate cash flows through all market cycles. Investors may have been spoiled over the last decade; simply throw money at any of the Mag 7 companies and make a positive return. When the easy money is off the table, this is where CGDG has the potential to thrive.

Underperformance Risks

However, there's always the risk that the expansion of the AI market continues once these current macro challenges pass. Although the market is currently questioning the high capex spend, we already know that sentiment can change very quickly. If the large-cap technology leaders do in fact deliver substantial earnings growth, we can see the positive momentum continue.

For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can expand to a value of ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% over the next few years. Even if this estimate is off by 50%, there's the chance that CGDG misses out on double-digit growth going forward. While CGDG does have some exposure through positions like TSM, it isn't structurally capable of fully participating in the upside growth.

Furthermore, the focus on sectors like financials and industrials means that the success of CGDG is sensitive to global economic growth. These are generally sectors that can be vulnerable to stagnation risks, such as lower consumer spending, infrastructure demand, and higher interest rates. The most notable barrier in the near term is the high-interest-rate environments that may lead to further deterioration within the debt markets. Despite the Fed initiating several cuts to interest rates throughout 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range.

Even during the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. While higher rates may lead to higher net interest margins on loans, this also shrinks the spread between what it earns on loans and what banks pay on deposits. Furthermore, companies that are reliant on debt financing to fuel operations may be facing increased pressure as a result of the higher costs.

Dividend

CGDG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of roughly 2% while issuing those payouts on a quarterly basis. However, the actual payout amounts will vary every quarter. Historically, the largest payout is in the middle of the year. For instance, both of the fund's June distributions were at the highest end of its range. These varying payouts mean that CGDG may not be the best choice for retired investors seeking a consistent stream of income that can efficiently support lifestyle expenses. The smaller dividend yield and focus on dividend growers make CGDG a better position for investors that may be approaching retirement or have a lower risk tolerance. By allowing a longer holding period, this allows compound interest to work its magic.

The magic is in the ETF's ability to provide dividend growth over time. Although the fund's history is short, CGDG has managed to provide a trailing twelve-month dividend growth rate of 9.39%. So investors that have held CGDG since inception would now have a yield on cost around 2.8%. With a few more years of consecutive dividend growth, CGDG can easily allow long-term investors to obtain a yield on cost above 5%. When looking at the top holdings, it is clear that CGDG should be able to maintain an attractive rate of dividend growth going forward:

  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing: 5Y Dividend CAGR of 13.95%.
  • Broadcom: maintains a very healthy payout ratio of 34.11% with 15 consecutive years of growth without interruption.
  • Welltower: has a low payout ratio of 50.08%, which is extremely healthy for a REIT.

Lastly, CGDG provides the benefit of issuing tax-efficient dividends to investors. The ETF structure means that most of the distributions will be classified as qualified dividends, which are taxed at the most favorable rates for investors. However, the fund's inclusion of foreign investments means that investors may see those foreign taxes withheld from a portion of the distributions. This may enable the possibility to claim foreign tax credits if CGDG is held within a regular taxable account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CGDG at this time. The fund's portfolio strategy has proven to be effective against the pullback of the indices. Although CGDG does have some technology exposure, management is very selective with what it holds, which has resulted in better capital preservation. Furthermore, the exclusion of high-growth, non-dividend-paying companies has worked out to its benefit. The ETF may be a great choice for investors that have a low-risk tolerance and want to offset uncertainty in the U.S. markets, higher interest rates, and a rotation out of technology. CGDG is also very likely to continue providing an attractive rate of dividend growth over the next few years.

data.title: The Market Is Early Calling The Peak In Micron
data.description:
I have been covering Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) since November last year. I remember concerns around high valuations and entering at highs at around $250, yet I felt a high conviction to Buy that was well supported. Similarly, at around $350, I felt the stock was becoming more volatile and the upside asymmetry was becoming riskier and hence recommended waiting for pullbacks for fresh positioning.

Micron, of course, belied expectations and rallied to over $470 before recent corrections brought it below prices where I had issued my downgrade. So, what does that mean for fresh entries at current levels, especially given my Hold rating was not too far off from levels we are seeing now? And more importantly, given the way Micron moves, $20-$30 moves are made within days, so the 5-10% net correction from the time of my last thesis may be stale even by the time this article gets published.

That means I have to answer the bigger question for the next big move - not a 5-10% target thesis that the gap between my last downgrade and current prices indicates. In this thesis, I try to answer whether Micron is likely to reach $400 earlier than $250 (enough room for the thesis to evolve beyond daily fluctuations). And this is what I find.

The demand story is a known support, and that continues to not be a factor in the near term, although long-term demand is a factor - it could actually prove that the cyclicality embedded in expectations today is more conservative and troughs in the AI era are higher than what has been seen earlier. A more important variable for Micron at the moment is the supply gap, and as of Q2 FY2026, even that remains supportive enough, as seen in pricing power and margin expansion. The only drag for Micron at the moment is a capex cyclicality risk and a guidance that conveys moderating incremental gains from here on (suggesting peak conditions are forming).

However, I feel that the timing for top formation and the amount of steam left in the cycle are being looked at conservatively. Operational constraints indicate supply cannot increase meaningfully in the near term, and there is enough time left for the supply to start responding in a way that starts to drag the pricing power. In other words, an opportunity seems to emerge from the way markets are correctly pricing in eventual normalization but potentially underestimating the trough depth and the duration over which elevated conditions can persist. That tells me we will hit $400 before we hit $250.

Further on in the thesis, I will discuss how the Buy trade is no longer as crowded as it was seemingly in January. Excess positioning has been removed, and volatility levels have compressed, which enables a fresh Buy not very far from the point of my last Hold. Interestingly, the thesis has evolved very quickly from Buy to a Hold and back to a Buy, all within a couple of months of each other - testimony to the magnitude of price moves Micron has been making of late.

A Recap Of Prior Calls

Not so long back in November last year, I felt markets were still mispricing the magnitude of demand and speed of earnings growth, alongside the supply constraints. I had modeled a DCF fair value of ~$380 but fully expected at least a few quarters to that target. What happened was growth was pulled forward, and margins also expanded beyond the near-term assumptions in my model. So, in January, I redrew the model with higher near-term growth assumptions and slower long-term growth numbers. Margins also had to be upgraded. The new fair value worked out to be around $420. I still called for a downgrade and advised waiting for fresh entries, although ~$70 of runway was implied as still left even as per my own model.

My downgrade to a Hold is therefore a reflection of how the thesis has moved from a high-conviction entry thesis to a mature and fulfilled thesis. Returns will come potentially with time and cash generation rather than rerating. The business may continue to exceed expectations, and momentum may still push Micron further up, but the price-value framework (relatively saturated now—within two months of the Buy) indicates the stock may not fundamentally price in any further execution surprises.

The Crowded Trade And Top Formation

One of the core reasons for my discomfort around the ~$350 levels was that the Buy trade was becoming more crowded, and Micron had started to make volatile and sharp big moves almost on a daily level, and on both sides. The 30-day rolling volatility chart below shows how, barring the close to 100% volatility levels seen around April 2025 (when Micron was less crowded below $100 and also going through a wider market volatility due to tariff uncertainties), the overall volatility trends were already in an uptrend by November last year (when I still found enough upside asymmetry to still issue a Buy). By December-January, not only had the asymmetry shrunk (a fair value of ~$380 as per my older model and ~$420 as per an upgraded model), but volatility levels had also shot up meaningfully from ~60% to ~75%. The combined effect of shrinking upside asymmetry and rising volatility was a no-go.

Another chart that highlights how the volatility and the magnitude of intraday moves have sharply increased over the past year is shown below. This one plots the absolute close-to-close % change in daily prices. This shows how the daily changes had moved from just over 2% a year ago to just under 4% by January. That amount of intraday moves and a rising 30-day volatility trend is often an indicator of a top forming in share prices and indicates crowded trades rather than fundamentals and long-term trends forming, in my view. In the recent sell-off, the volatility levels are back to ~60%, and on the way down, this is a lagging indicator - i.e. actual volatility should be lower than what we were seeing in November last year. This development removes one of the hurdles I had for fresh Buying at $350 in January this year. The rest of the asymmetry thesis is still pending, though.

Ditching The DCF Framework

The DCF modeling approach was still a reasonable approach in November because the forward distribution of outcome seemed linear and multiple quarters out. What has changed since is that both growth and margins have been pulled forward sharply, compressing the timing from a multi-quarter and multi-year cycle into a few concentrated quarters. As peak conditions start forming, cash flows are no longer smooth in the near term. Timing dependencies and terminal values are sensitive assumptions that affect the near-term share move versus a fair value estimate. Therefore, I move to a more qualitative thesis now with emphasis on the cycle duration outlook and supply-demand imbalance to see whether a Buy thesis now carries an edge over what the market is pricing in.

Where The Markets Are Potentially Wrong

The current consensus shows expectations of earnings peaking only in 2027. The quarterly rundown of that trajectory shows real slowdown starting to show up only by Q2 FY2027 (4 quarters away) and negative EPS growth from a year after. This assumes both a sharp demand trough (going as deep as ~$30 in the long term, by FY2029) as the AI capex cycle normalizes within those timeframes and a supply catching up with demand scenario.

My take is that the markets are correct on the direction but potentially wrong on the magnitude of normalization and the timing. Looking at the operational data and other indicators and how they have moved between Q1 and Q2 FY2026 helps understand why. Starting with pricing, because this is where the signal is cleanest in my view. DRAM prices were already reported to be strongly up by ~20% in Q1, but they have gone up much more sharply in Q2. DRAM prices were up by well over 60%, and NAND prices were reported to be up by a high-70% range. While a backward-looking indicator, it still shows that the supply imbalance is still extremely strong. Stronger pricing power has also meant gross margins have expanded significantly from ~57% in Q1 to ~75% now.

The forward outlook on pricing power and what is required for Micron to surprise the markets depends on the two important age-old variables - demand and supply. The demand thesis does not need an introduction for Micron. Management commentary in Q2 indicated they were able to meet 50% to two-thirds of the demand from key customers. Existing unmet demand is sufficient to keep the demand tight enough for pricing power. The supply side is what needs monitoring because that becomes the binding constraint.

On the supply side, Q2 commentary indicates that cleanroom limitations and node transitions continue to drag supply expansion bids. Management also said that industry supply could remain short of demand well beyond calendar year 2026. This in itself could mean the current trajectory assumes a sharp compression in EPS growth levels by Q2 FY2027. Capex trends show continued investments in FY2026 with expectations of above $25b for the year. However, with the current run rate, when extrapolated, it implies those levels roughly, so it is not a material ramp. FY2027 is expected to be a heavier ramp with a ramp of ~$10b in construction capex. The Idaho fab is expected to start initial wafer output only by mid-2027, and I believe other additions are even further out, supporting my immediate tight supply thesis - tight enough for longer to defy market expectations of a deceleration in EPS growth in 4 quarters from now.

The Q3 margin guidance of ~81% is still a further expansion, pushing Micron toward peak margin territory. And management has indicated moderating gains from there on. However, it does not affect the EPS trajectory assumed in the next few quarters. Capacity additions in the memory industry are a multi-year lead-time project, and immediate efficiency gains and higher production based on technology transitions have limited capacity to scale and address the magnitude of supply shortage at play. As the supply tightness looks worse than assumed, the EPS trajectory ahead will be able to remain strong even if pricing power and further margin gains remain limited. I expect volume growth (bit shipments still growing), mix shift (HBM and high-value products), and operating leverage to remain supportive.

Another potential mismatch in the market's long-term trough and terminal value assumptions could be the expected trough of ~$30 by FY2029 (from peak EPS of ~$100). This sharp drop potentially looks at an end date for the current capex cycle for training clusters. However, the extent of demand for inference loads is still speculative (and conservative looking at the sharp drop in EPS expectations). Depending on the number of users and queries, memory intensity is likely to rise per workload over time. Demand could also spill over from a handful of large hyperscalers to a broader base of users, devices, and enterprises. That upside of a consistent post-build phase is potentially not being factored into the cyclical EPS expectation model we have today.

Why $400 Before $250

Overall, for prices to go down further, we will need a multiple compression trajectory as earnings grow into valuations - going as sharply down as ~3x in forward earnings terms by FY2029. If we assume even a reasonable ~25% multiple expansion (~3.66x earnings), we get to $400 on multiple rerating alone. What actually supports an even higher target is an earnings surprise trajectory that extends beyond the next 4 quarters (as modeled today). If that happens, the upside opens up meaningfully well beyond $400. That path is highly possible looking at how the supply remains constrained longer than expected, a strong demand environment (strong enough scenarios also exist that see inference loads turning out stronger than the current cyclicality embedded in expectations). This path does not need much margin expansion or further gains in terms of pricing power. That path also enables some multiple expansion too, doubling the impact on returns.

The path to $200 needs a valuation sentiment drop due to a risk-off macro environment - that could probably still happen, but current forward valuations appear too low and require a lot of intrinsic factors to also play out. Supply should increase meaningfully, demand should not persist the way markets are factoring in, and margins should fall meaningfully too - something not indicated by the state we are in at the moment.

data.title: Tesla: This Is Not The Right Time To Buy The Dip
data.description:
Pivoting to Physical AI Comes at a Cost

Despite dropping 15% since the beginning of this year, Tesla, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:TSLA) stock is still a Strong Sell. I know this will agitate TSLA bulls, but trading at 175x forward P/E doesn't make the stock appealing at all. In particular, TSLA's quality could face more deteriorations in the near term due to heavy spending on "physical AI" in FY2026, which will cause negative FCF.

The company is now focused on Optimus and robotaxis over new model launches. The existing models have been losing market share.

Although the recent 4Q FY2025 earnings came in ahead of consensus, these numbers did not send a clear signal of growth inflection. You may argue that holding the stock is a bet on future large-scale monetization from AI, despite the harsh reality that its vehicle sales have declined in recent years.

Unless you choose to downplay financial statements and put your faith in the leadership's visions of TSLA's future businesses (which many people do), I still don't see a solid fundamental reason to own the stock.

Still a Tradable Stock

The stock's movement has been largely dominated by momentum chasing. We saw the price has broken a strong resistance of $391 in the November 2025 low, making it more vulnerable for further downside to $350. The downside could be exacerbated by a broad-based panic selloff from the recent geopolitical tensions.

I've been bearish on TSLA and maintained a Strong Sell since September 2025. I saw some comments in my last article saying that the stretched valuation won't matter; once the company reaches a growth inflection, the upside could be tremendous.

At that point, P/E will come down quickly. That sounds reasonable, but this will also expose a tremendous downside given that 175x forward P/E. When you pull out a 5-year chart, TSLA is only up 64% with huge volatility.

The company is expected to announce 1Q 2026 delivery results this Thursday. The consensus still suggests a 13% sequential growth decline compared to the previous quarter.

That said, TSLA is tradable but is not attractive to own. Buying at this valuation is premature and speculative. Therefore, despite a 4% bounce on Tuesday, I still don't think it's a good time to buy the dip, and I still maintain a Strong Sell.

What Drives the Price Higher?

Since TSLA decoupled from its fundamentals years ago, we can analyze what actually impacted the price movement over the past 5 years. One of the key catalysts was November 2025, when Trump was elected. The CEO, Elon Musk, as the largest donor, was closely tied to the administration. At the time, the stock jumped more than 50%, even though its 4Q FY2025 earnings had a double miss.

Another example was when the stock dipped +10% after Elon had a public feud with Trump back in June 2025. Later, the stock rebounded when the market started pricing in growth optimism around FSD and robotaxi, even though the latest earnings were still lackluster.

TSLA's upside potentials have not been driven by its financial statements. Even if we assume a massive future growth is achievable, much of it has already been priced in. You may ask why the stock remains resilient. That's how bubbles form.

Without actual growth feeding into the financials, the stock could be in a range bound for a long time.

Focus on Median Consensus, Not the Average

Last week, TSLA posted delivery consensus based on sell-side analysts in its press release; the standard deviations of estimates are so wide for several reasons. First, the company's delivery outlook has been inconsistent. Elon has guided for 50% long-term growth since 2020 and reiterated that outlook over the past few years. However, the company only achieved 15% CAGR from FY2021 to FY2025. Therefore, the consensus is controversial.

Second, despite losing market share in the EV market, some sell-side analysts remain extremely bullish on TSLA's growth outlook, setting very high TPs with aggressive delivery assumptions. These outliers can pull up the average numbers. I also believe the buy-side consensus is likely lower. For that reason, I prefer the median consensus.

1Q FY2026 Preview: No Inflection in Sight

The 1Q FY2026 delivery consensus median shows 363,371, growing 7.9% YoY. Looking at the chart, the company's delivery growth has been volatile and bouncing between negative and single-digit growth over the past two years. Even if the actual number comes in better than expected, it's not enough to drive a big positive surprise in revenue for 1Q FY2026.

On the earnings call, I believe management will continue focusing on AI developments, which is a double-edged sword in the near term because higher AI spending will pressure margins and bottom lines. Analysts will likely ask more questions about the AI spending outlook in FY2026 and beyond. I'll be watching the AI monetization side, including scale and timing.

FCF Will Turn Negative for the First Time Since FY2018

TSLA previously guided over $20 billion in capex in FY2026 as the company has shifted its focus from car sales to physical AI and automation, such as Optimus and Robotaxi. This strategic pivot could come under a challenge of increasing competition from its EV business, which has been declining.

Many AI companies have gone through similar transitions, resulting in sharp growth slowdowns, margin compressions, higher capex, and negative FCFs. Looking at the chart above, the capex is expected to jump 134.4% YoY, with the capex-to-total-sales ratio doubling to 19.3%. While this ratio doesn't look overreaching compared to 40%-50% among hyperscalers, the issue here is that TSLA's net income has declined by +20% YoY for three consecutive years. When the company significantly raises its capex outlook, FCF will easily turn deeply negative.

Let's do some calculations. The company's CFO margin remains stable at 15.3% and 15.6%, respectively, in FY2024 and FY2025. Assuming that it would expand to 16% in FY2026, we can estimate its FY2026 CFO would be around $16.5 billion, considering the $103.3 billion revenue consensus from Seeking Alpha. This means that +$20 billion in capex will at least cause a $4.5 billion decline in FCF in FY2026. This would mark the first year of negative FCF since FY2018.

Conclusion

I still believe the stock is in a bubble due to multi-year earnings declines. On top of that, a significant increase in AI robotics capex in FY2026 may lift the long-term growth outlook. In the near term, it will cost a sharp decline in FCF, which lowers the stock's quality.

Keep in mind, its valuation premium has already embedded a case that TSLA will be a future leader in AI robotics and automation. The company's strategic pivot to these areas has reflected this growth optimism. When you look at its car business, existing Tesla models will unlikely contribute much growth in the long run, especially after the removal of EV tax credits last year. So, from a risk and reward perspective, I stick to my Strong Sell rating on TSLA.

data.title: Why Investors Are Turning Bullish On Adeia: The Bull Case Explained
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The broader technology market has been under pressure due to tariff uncertainty, macroeconomic concerns, and rising rates. Adeia Inc. (ADEA) has done the opposite. The stock has increased by ~40% in the last three months, while the Nasdaq has declined by 10%. Isn't this worth paying attention to? This kind of outperformance does not happen without a good reason. In this case, the company has been signing multi-year IP licensing deals with some of the largest technology companies in the world.

In March 2026, Adeia signed a multi-year IP license agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). This followed the January 2026 multi-year agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) and the December 2025 long-term Media IP License deal with The Walt Disney Company (DIS). Each of these deals has two important characteristics that investors should consider.

First, all three are multi-year agreements. The exact duration is not disclosed, but in my understanding, multi-year typically means anywhere between three and ten years. In a nutshell, these deals will generate predictable recurring revenue for several years. Second, all three are with Tier-1 technology companies. That validates the quality and relevance of Adeia's patent portfolio in both the media and semiconductor industries.

Adeia is not a typical technology stock that makes products or sells software subscriptions. The company licenses its patent portfolio to companies operating in media technology, semiconductor design, and consumer electronics. The company was formed in October 2022 following the separation of Xperi Inc. (XPER).

Tier-1 Tech Deals: Disney, Microsoft, AMD, and What They Mean

Adeia signed a long-term IP license agreement with Disney for its media technology portfolio. This deal covers IP for media streaming and content delivery technology. With Microsoft, the company signed a multi-year IP license agreement covering the media portfolio and potentially semiconductors as well. The Microsoft deal was announced alongside the appointment of Craig Mitchell as Chief Semiconductor Officer and other key leadership additions. In my view, this is a clear signal that the company is looking for growth in the semiconductor sector. To support this, the company also signed a multi-year agreement with AMD.

For investors, it's important to gauge the impact of these deals on the fundamentals of the company and what connects these three deals. All three were signed in a span of a few months. Further, all are multi-year agreements with the capability to generate predictable recurring revenue. And most importantly, they are with companies that operate at the core of the technology ecosystem through their product offerings in AI compute, streaming media, and semiconductor design.

CEO Paul Davis confirmed in the Q4 2025 earnings call: "Our continued progress on diversifying our revenue base and expanding our customer reach in growth markets positions us well for the years ahead."

As per my analysis, these deals indicate a broader trend of licensing activity for the company, which is confirmed by the 13% growth in the patent portfolio for FY 2025. A validation by the larger companies confirmed the relevance of Adeia's patent portfolio, which will create more opportunities for licensing and a potential for upside.

AMD Deal and Semiconductor Pivot: The Most Important Near-Term Catalyst

As I have highlighted, in January 2026, the company expanded its leadership team to accelerate growth in semiconductors. In March 2026, the company finalized the multi-year deal with AMD, which signifies a strategy to focus on the semiconductor sector. This is the most important development in the Adeia story right now.

In November 2025, Adeia filed ten patent infringement lawsuits against AMD in the US District Court for the Western District of Texas. The patents covered semiconductor innovations in hybrid bonding technology and advanced process node technology. These are technologies that are directly relevant to how AI chips are designed and manufactured.

For years, AMD’s products have incorporated and made extensive use of Adeia’s patented semiconductor innovations, which have greatly contributed to their success as a market leader. After prolonged efforts to reach a mutually agreeable resolution without litigation, we believe this step was necessary to defend our intellectual property from AMD’s continued unauthorized use." said CEO Paul Davis.

Interestingly, in the span of a few months of the lawsuit being filed, AMD signed a multi-year IP license agreement with Adeia. As per my analysis, this is the strong indicator of patent validity. It strengthens Adeia's position in every future semiconductor licensing conversation with new customers.

The semiconductor licensing business at Adeia is still small. But it is growing fast, and the trajectory is compelling.

Data in $million FY2023 FY2024 FY2025
Revenue 13 18 26
Growth 38% 40%

Compiled by the author using data from Earnings Transcript Q4 2025.

Semiconductor revenue grew 40% in FY 2025 to $26 million from $18 million in FY 2024. Going forward, the recurring revenue from deals with AMD and Microsoft will be added to revenue. The reason for bringing up this data point is to highlight the potential of Adeia as a part of emerging technologies of the future and as the growth engine investors should track over the next 2–3 years.

IP Licensing Market: A Growing Addressable Opportunity

Adeia operates in the global IP licensing market, which will grow as technological innovation accelerates globally. According to Strategic Market Research, the global IP licensing market is estimated to increase from $13.9 billion in 2024 to $21.6 billion by 2030, at a CAGR of 7.5%. The growth drivers are structural, where AI drives new IP categories, and this will create new IP licensing opportunities. The semiconductor industry requires specialized process patents, and Adeia's IP patent portfolio is expanding. In a nutshell, Adeia's patent portfolio covers all booming sectors, i.e., streaming media, AI semiconductors, and consumer electronics. Investors should consider this point while evaluating this company.

Still Trading at a Discount Despite the Rally

Despite the rally in the last 3 months, it still trades at 16.10x forward P/E on FY 2026 EPS, a 22% discount to the sector median of 20.39x. InterDigital (IDCC) is the most direct comparable peer, trading at 29.91x. At that multiple on FY 2026 EPS of $1.44, the implied stock price will be $43.25 against the current price of $23.13. Given the multiple deals the company has completed in a few months, I believe the stock deserves the premium compared to its peers.

Final Thoughts

The company is signing multi-year deals with Disney, Microsoft, and now AMD. Further, the patent portfolio is growing at 13% annually. In my view, the stock is cheap in comparison to its growth potential in media and semiconductors. In a nutshell, this is a high-margin business with a growing patent portfolio and a record of new customer trading at a discount. Investors should watch Q1 2026 results closely for the contribution from the AMD deal.

data.title: Diversification By Design: Tapping The Return Potential Of Everyday Cash Flows
data.description:
By James Sackett & Neil Hepworth

Curating diverse return streams is important. Asset-based finance may help.

Diversification is often treated as an asset-allocation decision. How much capital should go to equities? Bonds? Alternatives? But portfolio design matters, too, and this is where we think asset-based finance can play a crucial role.

Rather than relying on return correlations among traditional investment categories, asset-based finance seeks to generate returns by securitizing the cash flows from a broad array of income-generating assets backed by diverse collateral. These include auto loans, mortgages and other forms of consumer debt.

Investments are secured by hundreds—and sometimes thousands—of loans and other tangible, income-generating assets that produce contractual cash flows according to different timelines. And because the loans are self-amortizing, they repay principal gradually over time, becoming less risky as they do.

That’s distinct from the way corporate loans typically work: they pay regular interest but don’t repay any principal until the end of the loan term.

As we see it, these features may help to generate steady returns at a time when late-cycle dynamics and AI-related anxiety threaten to push investors into making decisions based on fear, not fundamentals.

Asset-Based Finance’s Origin Story

In the past, borrowers turned to banks for this type of financing. But tighter regulations have pushed much of that activity to specialty finance companies. These nonbank lenders don’t have balance sheets big enough to hold all the loans they originate, so they partner with private credit investors.

In practice, that means specialty lenders source, originate and service the loans, while private credit investors set the parameters for borrowers and collateral types, provide the capital and own the loans.

There is more than $6 trillion of debt in the specialty finance ecosystem today, making it the primary source of lending for the real economy. We expect the size of the market to exceed $10 trillion before the end of the decade—an increase that has yet to be fully reflected in investor allocations.

Diversified Sources and Investor-Friendly Structuring

The breadth of cash flows and the backing collateral are key strengths, in our view. Residential mortgages, consumer installment loans, point-of-sale finance and other contractual payment streams all generate income from different sources, over different time horizons and with different sensitivities to economic conditions.

What’s more, asset-based loan portfolios can be structured to be “bankruptcy remote.” This means they’re not tied to a company’s financial performance, including the risk of corporate default (Display).

The Nuts and Bolts of Asset-Based Finance

For example, if an auto financing company fails, asset-based investors would still receive cash flows from any loans they own, which sit in a separate legal entity. And the cars would continue to serve as collateral.

Tapping into a Varied Opportunity Set

The opportunity set is vast, particularly in the United States and Europe, where private asset-based credit is funding multiple forms of consumer spending and helping to address structural housing shortages.

The flexibility of the asset class provides an array of tools with which managers can shape cash flows from a wide range of income-generating assets into steady and diversified income streams with low correlation to other strategies—and to each other. The goal is to engineer a portfolio that generates a steady, curated set of cash flows throughout an economic cycle.

Access to various types of lending allows managers to tilt exposure across the span of the cycle as growth, credit availability and borrower behavior changes. This might mean leaning into subprime auto loans early in the cycle when access to credit is expanding but tilting toward residential mortgages and other seasoned assets in the later stages.

A Strategy Adaptable to the Economic Cycle

Most investments are backed by amortizing assets with relatively short average-weighted lives. That’s important because it removes the need to try to call the economic cycle before committing capital. Even in the late stages of a credit cycle, conditions typically weaken gradually.

This doesn’t mean that lenders ignore the cycle entirely. But the granularity and short-dated nature of these loans have the potential to act as a built-in buffer, allowing underwriting criteria to be adjusted quickly in a constant feedback loop.

For example, a pool of consumer loans originated in March may be underwritten with return expectations based on the performance of thousands of similar existing loans. If the economic outlook changes in April enough to alter likely performance, the underwriting standards lenders apply to the next pool of loans can be adapted. This enables continued investment throughout the cycle, and the short-term nature of most loans means they repay quickly.

In our view, this makes asset-based lending a strong complement to the direct-lending strategies that dominate many private credit allocations. Most privately originated loans to middle-market companies come with five-to-seven-year maturities designed to match the typical hold periods of the borrowers’ private-equity owners. It isn’t uncommon for several loans to require refinancing at or around the same time.

Control Through Deal Structuring

Other types of deal structuring can also play important roles. For example, portfolios are often structured to be overcollateralized. This means the total value of the cash flow the underlying loans generate is greater than the amount of debt issued against those assets.

Incentive structures for the originator also help to reduce risk. A transaction might be built so that the originators of a pool of auto loans only receive some or all of their fees after investors have received interest and principal. Another might require the originator to absorb losses first, a potentially valuable feature in the later stages of an economic cycle.

On the other hand, a lower-risk pool of assets, such as seasoned prime residential mortgages with low loan-to-value ratios, might provide an opportunity for investors to lean in the other direction by taking an equity position in the capital structure to boost return potential.

None of this means asset-based finance is immune to volatility or losses. But the granular nature of lending and the levers managers can pull to generate continuous amortizing cash flows may generate strong return potential in all market environments while diversifying exposure to other forms of public and private credit.

By focusing on how cash flows are created and prioritized, asset-based strategies allow investors to think about diversification as a design exercise, not just an allocation choice.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boston Scientific: From Hypergrowth To Durable Compounding
data.description:
Boston Scientific (BSX), maker of popular medical devices such as WATCHMAN, has been punished by the market over the last six months, with stock down more than 35%. This is partly due to rerating as growth is slowing from its peak and starting to normalize. As a result, multiples versus growth expectations are being recalibrated. However, the business is still enjoying sustained double-digit growth and operating margins of 28%, and the selloff may be out of proportion compared to the slowdown. The company offers higher-than-peer growth at a fair valuation. With several catalysts in the pipeline, including WATCHMAN CHAMPION, I rate Boston Scientific a Buy.

Growth Profile: Deceleration, Not Breakdown

Management was justifiably happy with the numbers for FY and Q4 2025. In the words of Michael Mahoney, “We're super pleased with the quarter and the full year.” Full-year revenue came in at $20 billion, with operational revenue growth of 19.2% and organic revenue growth of 15.8%. For Q4, operational revenue was up 14.3%, at the higher end of guidance. With acquisitions contributing 160 basis points, organic revenue growth for the quarter came in at 12.7%. Importantly, revenue growth is substantially above pre-COVID levels, with revenue growth of 9.3% in 2019.

Full-year adjusted EPS of $3.06 topped guidance of $3.02 to $3.04, representing a 22% increase, on top of a 100 basis point improvement in adjusted operating margin to 28%. Electrophysiology grew by 35% in Q4, followed by WATCHMAN, which grew by 29% and is seeing increased international adoption. For full-year 2026, the company is guiding towards 10.5-11.5%, which does mark a legitimate slowdown. Full-year adjusted EPS at the midpoint of $3.46 would mark an increase of 13%, indicating a modest margin expansion and generally in line with our thesis of normalization.

Valuation Reset Creates Relative Opportunity

The 35% drop over the last six months has created a compelling multiple situation looking ahead. According to Seeking Alpha figures, analysts are estimating EPS of $3.91 in 2027 and $4.41 in 2028, both representing year-over-year growth of around 12.7%. This results in a forward P/E of 16.1x for 2027 and 14.3x for 2028, which can be considered underpriced if growth holds up.

Meanwhile, the market appears to be pricing Boston Scientific as a mid-growth company. At 18x FY1 earnings, it’s more expensive than Medtronic (MDT) and cheaper than Edwards Lifesciences (EW). Forward EV/EBITDA paints a similar picture, with BSC sitting at 15.4 versus Medtronic’s 12.8 and Edwards’ 20.4. However, look at the growth you’re buying at this price.

Boston Scientific’s 5-year revenue CAGR of 15.1% is vastly higher than its competitors at 4.9% and 6.7%, respectively. The same is true for the 3-year EBITDA CAGR, which at 19% towers above the competition, which is at 3.4% and 2.2%. Finally, the quality of the earnings is better, with a 3-year CAGR FCF of 41%, again considerably better than MDT’s 8.5% and EW’s 14.6%. Essentially, you’re paying a mid-tier valuation for meaningfully higher growth.

Sustaining Above-Market Growth

The question now becomes whether BSX is able to sustain this above-market growth rate, and there are several reasons to believe this is the case. Electrophysiology remains a structural growth driver, with Q4 growth of 35%, well above the market average of 18-20%. The market is expected to keep growing by 15% in 2026, and management has indicated they are confident that they can continue to outpace the overall market.

What could really drive growth is the WATCHMAN CHAMPION trial. WATCHMAN currently has an addressable market of around 5 million patients, but a successful expansion would quadruple this to 20 million patients. This would boost already strong growth of 29% and move the product from niche use to first-line therapy. However, it’s important to keep in mind that this is as of yet unproven.

Thirdly, the company is betting hard on M&A to enter into high-growth adjacent markets, and acquisitions like Penumbra and Valencia are already contributing meaningfully to the top line. As mentioned, acquisitions contributed 160 basis points in the most recent earnings report. Total cash and ST equivalents of $1.965 combined with net leverage of around 1.9 leaves room for further acquisitions.

In short, growth is not dependent on a single variable. EP, WATCHMAN, and segment expansion into other fields like oncology and M&A are all viable growth drivers, and together, they suggest that the company can at least keep up EPS growth estimates of around 11% per year as margins expand.

Risks & Catalysts

The primary risk to the Buy thesis is that revenue and EPS growth disappoint. This would mean that current forward multiples would start to look more optimistic. Specifically, if revenue and EPS growth drop into single digits, the normalization narrative would come under pressure, and structural decline would become a more accurate descriptor.

Also, already high margins leave less room for expansion, with operating margin already at 28%. The company’s dependency on EP and WATCHMAN means that execution here is key, and while diversification will help, investors will mostly be looking at the core business. If the results of the CHAMPION trial are unsuccessful, the addressable patient base will remain around 5 million.

This is also the potential upside. If trials are successful, this could lead to a massive revenue boost in a core business, which would help drive earnings growth as well. At the same time, recovery in weaker segments like urology, CRM, and neuromod would boost investor confidence. More news of M&A and specifically their contributions to the top line would also be a potential catalyst.

Conclusion

After a 35% drop in the past six months, Boston Scientific has undergone a significant multiple reset. Growth is clearly slowing down from peak levels, but it remains robust and durable. At current levels, the market seems to be pricing Boston Scientific as a mid-growth medtech company, but a closer look at the comps reveals that it's growing considerably faster than the competition. Essentially, Boston Scientific is transitioning from a hypergrowth company to a sustainable compounder, and it appears that the current valuation does not fully reflect earnings growth durability.

data.title: Dana Incorporated: Fundamentals Have Improved A Lot
data.description:
Investment Overview

I wrote about Dana Incorporated (DAN) previously with a buy rating in my initiation coverage, as I thought the business was in much better shape after the divestiture, had a path towards double-digit EBITDA margin, and also a de-risked near-term outlook. The developments since my last update have been largely positive. The balance sheet reset is done, margin improvement is now showing up in the numbers, and the growth story looks broader than before as ICE-related demand, backlog, and aftermarket momentum all improve. Therefore, I reiterate: buy.

My Old Bull Case Is Now Showing Up In The Numbers

There were two main points in my initiation coverage thesis: (1) the off-highway sale would fix the balance sheet, and (2) the cost program would lift margins. All of these are now visible in the numbers and in how management is framing 2026.

DAN finally closed the Off-Highway divestiture on 1st Jan this year, and it has already completed ~$2 billion of debt reduction. The latest cash position, as of the end of January, was $659 million, and liquidity was ~$1.8 billion. An important commentary made was that net leverage should stay <1x through 2026, which directly supports my view that this divestiture would fix the balance sheet. Next, the point on margin improvement also looks much more credible now if you look at the FY2025 numbers. While sales were down y/y to $7.5 billion, adj. EBITDA went up sharply to $610 million, with adj. EBITDA margin up to 8.1%. The numbers were even better if you just look at the Q4 numbers, in that adj. EBITDA margin reached 11.1% vs. 4.7% in Q4 2024. Note that this was driven mostly by cost savings and efficiency gains (as I said previously) and not entirely because of better volumes.

For the 2026 guidance, management guided to $7.3 billion to $7.7 billion of sales and $750 million to $850 million of adj. EBITDA. If you do the math, that implies a full-year adj. EBITDA margin of 10.6%, which tells us that this margin expansion story still relies largely on initiatives. And that also means that there is room for more expansion once revenue starts to scale up.

Growth Narrative

This part of the equity story has gotten much better as well. At the Capital Markets Day (CMD), management said only ~15% of quoting activity in 2023 was traditional or ICE-related because the industry was heavily tilted toward EV programs. Fast forward 3 years later, ~75% of current quote activity is ICE-related, and that is a significant swing. Management also said customers are revisiting product plans, extending ICE programs, and, in some cases, even launching new ICE vehicles. This goes against the narrative that “Electric Vehicles (EVs) will entirely displace ICE vehicles in the near term," and it matters a lot for DAN, as its legacy driveline, thermal, and sealing content is built for that kind of demand.

The base business growth profile also looks better. Specifically, Super Duty, which is DAN’s largest revenue program, has now been secured through 2038. Wrangler is also secured through 2029, and the JLR truck portfolio is secured even further through 2036. In other words, there is a very visible line of sight to the mid- and long-term growth, with potential upside when more ICE vehicle production is coming up. Then, you have the backlog to add more visibility to this growth narrative. DAN’s three-year new business backlog is now $750 million, up 33% y/y, and that is going to grow to $1.15 billion if we look through 2030.

Aftermarket

I would also add a stronger case that DAN can add growth in areas that make the growth story more durable and also with higher margins. The one area I would focus on is aftermarket. Per management, DAN’s global aftermarket business already generates more than $850 million of annual turnover, and that is split approximately 60/40 between Light Vehicle and Commercial Vehicle and is concentrated mainly in North America and EMEA. That may seem small now relative to the FY30 revenue target, but the growth runway here is huge. The estimate is that this addressable aftermarket market is worth ~$6.6 billion (per the CMD). I believe we would see strong growth here in the coming quarters/years, as, over the last six months, DAN has secured contracts with AutoZone, NAPA, and Advance Auto Parts. For reference, these three players represent >17,000 storefronts across the US and Canada.

Some might also think that this is not going to be a meaningful earnings growth driver given the size of the revenue, but remember, aftermarket usually carries a better margin profile and is less tied to new vehicle production. The 2030 guide provided was that the aftermarket can achieve a 32.5% adj. EBITDA margin, so even at this current $850 million, at that level of profitability, it translates to $276 million of adj. EBITDA, which would be close to 50% of FY2025 adj. EBITDA.

Valuation

After a solid share price rally since last August (when the stock was at ~14x NTM PE), I think the current valuation (~11x NTM PE) is another attractive entry point for investors. My new way of valuing DAN is by anchoring it against the FY30 target laid out at the CMD. In the CMD, management guided to $10 billion in revenue. From there, the question is what margins DAN would be able to achieve. DAN did achieve an adj. net margin of ~1.1% in FY25, and if we assume a high drop-through from adj. EBITDA margin expansion to the bottom line (assuming no step-up in leverage), which is about ~180 bps (management guided for a 10.7% adj. EBITDA margin vs. FY24 of 8.9%), we can bridge to DAN achieving ~3% or more adj. net margin by FY30 (assuming more fixed-cost leverage). Say that DAN exits at a 4% adj. net margin; that equates to FY30 adj. earnings of $400 million. Even without multiples expanding back to 14x, the stock is worth ~$4.4 billion market cap, or $40.

Risk

The risk is that the market may lose patience before DAN starts showing the strong earnings growth (i.e., wait till 2030), since the 2026 guide still assumes roughly flat sales. While I am bullish on the cost savings side of things, that also means there is limited potential for a sharp earnings growth acceleration in 2026 and maybe even early 2027. In the worst case, if DAN faces an unexpected cost increase and margins decline, the market may stop giving credit for the FY30 framework.

Conclusion

I maintain a buy rating on DAN. Fast forward to today, the balance sheet has already been repaired, margin expansion is already visible, and the growth drivers are more visible in that DAN now has multiple ways to grow earnings from here. I believe this makes the current setup attractive.

data.title: Invesco Global Focus Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 The fund lagged its benchmark for the quarter but exceeded peers.

Class A shares underperformed the MSCI ACWI Growth Index for the quarter but exceeded the peer group average, ranking in the 40 th percentile (147 of 314) of the Morningstar Global Large-Stock Growth category.

2 US stocks lagged non-US stocks; growth stocks underperformed

Global equities rose, led by emerging market and European equities, as Asia's technology-driven gains offset late-quarter weakness in US technology stocks.

3 Positioned for upside in 2026

Looking to 2026, we believe a supportive macroeconomic backdrop and easing monetary policy support our constructive outlook for global equities. We believe the most compelling opportunities are in high quality companies and leading AI innovators.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global equities were positive for the quarter, with international equities outperforming the US as late-quarter concerns about elevated technology valuations appeared to temper AI-driven momentum. Emerging markets led due to a broad Asian technology rally and robust results in South Korea; China lagged due to weak economic data and currency strength. Europe also delivered solid returns, supported by a stronger euro, improving manufacturing trends and expectations of fiscal stimulus in Germany.
  • Looking to 2026, we maintain a constructive outlook for global equities, supported by reduced trade policy uncertainty, firmer growth forecasts and expected monetary easing led by the US Federal Reserve, despite cooling in the US labor market. Risks such as slower growth, renewed inflation and geopolitics have persisted, but we do not expect them to materially alter our long-term investment views.
  • In this environment, we still favor high quality companies with good balance sheets, attractive profit margins and solid cash-generation capabilities. We see greater vulnerabilities in private markets and lower quality AI-themed companies reliant on debt-funded spending. We believe public markets will keep rewarding data center hyperscalers and AI innovators able to self-fund investment, with the most compelling long-term opportunities among companies owning leading semiconductor intellectual property or advanced large language models.

Portfolio positioning

Invesco Global Focus Fund is a high conviction portfolio with a bottom-up approach to stock selection. We believe consistent, long-term outperformance can best be achieved by investing within select long-term thematic trends, or tailwinds. A growing ecosystem with some advantaged participants is a good starting point for security level research, in our view, and that is where we direct our efforts.

We initiated one new position during the quarter.

Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) is a leading medical device company providing robotic-assisted surgery systems. Intuitive is most known for the da Vinci Surgical System that enables surgeons to perform minimally invasive procedures with enhanced precision, vision and control. We believe Intuitive offers a compelling value proposition at the intersection of cutting-edge medical technology and the needs of an aging global population.

We sold three positions during the quarter.

Zoetis (ZTS) specializes in animal health. We sold the position after management reported weak quarterly results and attributed the shortfall to a challenging macroeconomic environment and fewer veterinary visits. Competitors have not experienced similar issues, which makes us skeptical of the explanation. After Zoetis lowered its annual revenue forecast due to muted demand, we redeployed the capital into investments we believe offer more compelling return profiles.

Ferrari (RACE) is an Italian luxury car manufacturer. We sold the position after a meeting with management. We believe Ferrari has focused too much on personalization and hybrid vehicles, which historically tend to have lower resale values and, consequently, weaker demand. Management has since identified this issue and has been moving to scale back hybrids in favor of internal combustion engine cars but is not expecting much growth during the transition.

NiCE (NICE) is an enterprise software company serving customer engagement, financial crime and compliance markets. Management recently issued a weaker profitability forecast for 2026, citing heavy investment in AI and declines in revenue and net income. Also, NiCE has been considering the sale of Actimize, its profitable financial crime and compliance division, to help fund its acquisition of AI startup Cognigy, a move investors appear to have viewed cautiously. We are also increasingly concerned that NiCE's business is vulnerable to AI disruption, particularly from Anthropic (not a fund holding). Given these headwinds, we sold the position during the quarter.

Performance highlights

An overweight in health care, stock selection in financials and an underweight in information technology added to relative return.

The fund underperformed most in communication services and industrials due to stock selection and in consumer discretionary due to stock selection and an overweight.

Contributors to performance

Below are the largest contributors to relative return for the quarter:

Microsoft stock declined on concerns about rising AI capital spending and slower product uptake. The fund is underweight relative to the benchmark because we remain cautious about its valuation despite AI investment capacity. The underweight added to relative return.

Alphabet had a strong quarter with revenue topping $100 billion, driven by AI demand, Google Cloud growth and momentum in search advertising. TPU (tensor processing unit) chip sales and the success of Gemini Pro 3 in our view reinforced Alphabet's AI leadership and it remains the fund's largest holding.

Thermo Fisher Scientific shares rose following a better-than-expected quarterly earnings report in which it raised revenue forecasts. Recent acquisitions and strategic collaborations highlighted in our view the company's improved positioning moving forward.

Eli Lilly had a robust quarter due to soaring demand for GLP-1 obesity drugs. Management raised guidance and the stock reached a $1 trillion market cap. Amid growing optimism for its upcoming oral GLP-1 drug, we trimmed the position after strong gains.

Lam Research beat revenue and earnings expectations due to strong demand for advanced semiconductor tools as its etch and deposition technologies gained traction across next-generation chip designs.

Detractors from performance

Below are the largest detractors from relative return for the quarter:

Meta Platform stock fell on concerns about AI spending and a one-time tax charge, though we believe returns on AI investment remain strong. Our thesis is intact, but we trimmed the position in an effort to balance risk and reward.

Uber beat earnings estimates during the quarter, but operating income was below expectations. Communication regarding updated profit margin guidance also appeared to create some confusion.

ServiceNow faced industry headwinds and a government shutdown during the quarter. Its acquisition of Veza bolstered its AI portfolio but created concern about integration risk.

Tencent shares experienced volatility despite good quarterly earnings. Like many Chinese equities, the company faced tariff-related headwinds. We believe Tencent has demonstrated its ability to generate attractive returns on AI capital spending.

Netflix shares declined during the quarter due to earnings that fell short of expectations and its $82.7 billion bid to purchase Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD). We believe this is a very significant financial investment and, in our opinion, not one management would make unless it were struggling to grow user engagement organically.

The fund seeks capital appreciation.

About Risk

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging markets, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The performance of an investment concentrated in issuers of a certain region or country is expected to be closely tied to conditions within that region and to be more volatile than more geographically diversified funds.

Growth stocks tend to be more sensitive to changes in their earnings and can be more volatile.

Many countries in the European Union are susceptible to high economic risks associated with high levels of debt, notably due to investments in sovereign debts of European countries such as Greece, Italy, and Spain.

The Fund's value may be affected by changes in the stock markets. Stock markets may experience significant short-term volatility and may fall or rise sharply at times. Adverse events in any part of the equity or fixed-income markets may have unexpected negative effects on other market segments. Different stock markets may behave differently from each other and U.S. stock markets may move in the opposite direction from one or more foreign stock markets.

The investment techniques and risk analysis used by the portfolio managers may not produce the desired results.

Stocks of small and medium-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

Investing in securities of Chinese companies involves additional risks, including, but not limited to: the economy of China differs, often unfavorably, from the U.S. economy in such respects as structure, general development, government involvement, wealth distribution, rate of inflation, growth rate, allocation of resources and capital reinvestment, among others; the central government has historically exercised substantial control over virtually every sector of the Chinese economy through administrative regulation and/or state ownership; and actions of the Chinese central and local government authorities continue to have a substantial effect on economic conditions in China.

A value style of investing is subject to the risk that the valuations never improve or that the returns will trail other styles of investing or the overall stock markets.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund's financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.
